metadata.title: Why the Same Drug That Kills Cancer Cells Kills Platelets (and How to Fix It)
metadata.purpose: Vox-explainer slate cut of Cancer Biology candidate 03. Demonstrates that the therapeutic window of a BH3 mimetic is set not by drug potency but by which normal cells share the same guardian dependency as the tumor.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on real newsprint scan; desaturated plates; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, teal = BCL-2-dependent / spared / survives, crimson = BCL-XL-dependent / killed / dies, gold highlighter as the single editor's-pen voice (fill only, never text)

metadata.color_semantics: TEAL #1F6F5C = BCL-2-dependent cell / survives venetoclax / therapeutic success; CRIMSON #BF3339 = BCL-XL-dependent / killed by navitoclax / on-target toxicity. Never swap mid-film. GOLD = single editor's-pen highlight (fill only).
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO full intrinsic pathway mechanics, NO MCL-1 inhibitors or cardiotoxicity, NO IAP antagonists or TRAIL agonists, NO BH3 profiling assay detail, NO trial data tables. The manim move is 'compare' — the 2x3 cell-fate grid is the visual through-line. Three illustrative cell fates: CLL leukemia cell (BCL-2-dependent), platelet (BCL-XL-dependent), neutrophil (neither). All illustrative figures are labeled as such in FACTCHECK.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Pragmatist register (voices/pragmatist/VOICE.md + HAI.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the HAI outro from AUTHOR.MD :: Humanitarians AI
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: A drug that releases the death program cancer cells suppressed shows real anti-tumor activity in trials — then causes severe platelet destruction in the same patients.

card.copy: Why the Same Drug That Kills Cancer Cells Kills Platelets

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] title with CANCER BIOLOGY eyebrow in teal

beats.narration_text: Navitoclax entered CLL trials with demonstrated tumor activity. Platelet counts then fell severely — not a rare adverse event, a consistent dose-limiting toxicity.

props.headline: Navitoclax was biologically elegant — it entered clinical trials for leukemia,…

beats.scene_description: A microscope blood smear showing platelets — small disk-like fragments — alongside white blood cells, on aged newsprint, desaturated editorial treatment, push-in on the platelet field
beats.image_prompt: B02, blood smear under microscope showing platelets as small disc-shaped fragments among white blood cells, high magnification, clinical laboratory photography, desaturated editorial treatment pinned to aged newsprint, flat print reproduction

beats.narration_text: Navitoclax inhibits BCL-2 and BCL-XL to release the death program cancer cells have suppressed. It kills the leukemia cells. It also kills platelets. The mechanism is the same in both cases. Why?

card.copy: A BCL-2/BCL-XL inhibitor releases the death program cancer cells have suppressed.
card.sub: Why do platelets die from the same drug?

beats.narration_text: The standard selectivity assumption: a drug kills cancer cells because those cells express the target and normal cells do not. BCL-2 is overexpressed in CLL — so BCL-2 blockade should be selective. That assumption fails here.

production_viz.label: the naive model: cancer has BCL-2, normal cells lack it
production_viz.mechanic: two columns labeled 'LEUKEMIA CELL' (teal) and 'NORMAL CELL' (slate); a teal bar labeled BCL-2 fills the left column; the right column is empty; arrow from drug to leukemia cell only

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] naive model — drug targets BCL-2 in cancer only

beats.narration_text: BCL-2 family proteins are survival guardians. They sequester the proteins that would otherwise open mitochondrial pores and execute the cell. Cancer cells overexpress these guardians to hold the death program in check.

production_viz.label: guardian holds the death program at bay
production_viz.mechanic: a slate-teal card labeled 'GUARDIAN' (BCL-2) sits at center; a crimson block labeled 'death program' is held against a boundary; an arrow from the guardian to the block shows suppression

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] guardian concept — holding death in check

beats.narration_text: Multiple guardians exist: BCL-2, BCL-XL, MCL-1. Each cell type relies on a different one. The CLL cell depends on BCL-2. The platelet depends on BCL-XL. Different cells, different guardian dependencies.

production_viz.label: three guardians, each used by different cells
production_viz.mechanic: three chips labeled 'BCL-2', 'BCL-XL', 'MCL-1' arranged horizontally; arrows from each to different cell-type chips below: BCL-2 to CLL, BCL-XL to Platelet, MCL-1 to others (muted/faded)

beats.narration_text: Navitoclax blocks both BCL-2 and BCL-XL. In the leukemia cell: BCL-2 inhibition releases the death program — therapeutic. In the platelet: BCL-XL inhibition releases the same program — on-target toxicity. Both cells die by the same mechanism.

production_viz.label: navitoclax hits both BCL-2 and BCL-XL
production_viz.mechanic: a horizontal bar representing navitoclax sits at top; two lines descend, one to BCL-2 (teal), one to BCL-XL (crimson becomes active) — both hit; below BCL-2: CLL cell dies (good, teal); below BCL-XL: Platelet dies (bad, crimson)

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] navitoclax dual hit — both cell types die

beats.narration_text: This is on-target toxicity — not an off-target side effect. The drug did exactly what it was designed to do. Platelets require BCL-XL for survival. Blocking BCL-XL killed them on mechanism.

card.copy: On-target toxicity in a cell that shares the dependency.
card.sub: not a side effect — the mechanism working as designed

beats.narration_text: The therapeutic window is not set by how potently the drug kills the tumor. It is set by whether any normal cell that cannot be lost shares the same guardian dependency. That is the constraint to map before clinical use.

production_viz.label: therapeutic window = dependency gap, not potency
production_viz.mechanic: two overlapping rectangles: left = 'CANCER CELL BCL-2 dependency' (teal), right = 'PLATELET BCL-XL dependency' (crimson); when the drug overlaps both (a wide bar) the overlap zone is the problem; narrow bar hits only the left

beats.narration_text: The fix was structural redesign. Venetoclax was engineered to bind BCL-2 with 500-fold greater affinity than BCL-XL. At therapeutic doses, BCL-XL in platelets is not meaningfully inhibited. The platelet's guardian is spared.

production_viz.label: venetoclax: BCL-2 selective, BCL-XL spared
production_viz.mechanic: two guardians BCL-2 (teal filled) and BCL-XL (slate, faded); venetoclax arrow hits BCL-2 strongly (bold teal line), barely reaches BCL-XL (dashed, thin); '500x selectivity' label on the gap

beats.narration_text: Three cell types, same drug dose. A CLL leukemia cell is BCL-2-dependent. A platelet is BCL-XL-dependent. A neutrophil depends on neither at survival-critical levels — it holds reserve guardians.

production_viz.label: three cell types, their guardian dependency shown
production_viz.mechanic: three columns: CLL cell (teal card, BCL-2 chip lit), Platelet (crimson card, BCL-XL chip lit), Neutrophil (slate card, both chips dim); each column's dependency chip is highlighted

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] three cell types with guardian dependency chips

beats.narration_text: Add navitoclax. CLL cell: BCL-2 blocked — death program executes. Platelet: BCL-XL also blocked — same execution, same outcome. Neutrophil: remaining guardians hold — survives. Three different fates from one drug. This is illustrative.

production_viz.label: navitoclax fates: CLL dies (good), platelet dies (bad), neutrophil lives
production_viz.mechanic: same three-column layout from B11; navitoclax bar appears above all three; CLL column resolves to crimson 'DIES' chip (therapeutic); Platelet resolves to crimson 'DIES' chip (toxicity); Neutrophil resolves to teal 'SURVIVES' chip

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] navitoclax fates across three cell types

beats.narration_text: Switch to venetoclax, same dose. BCL-2 blocked, BCL-XL spared. CLL cell still dies — its dependency is still targeted. Platelet's BCL-XL guardian is intact — platelet survives. Neutrophil unchanged. One structural change opened the therapeutic window. Illustrative.

production_viz.label: venetoclax fates: CLL dies, platelet survives, neutrophil survives
production_viz.mechanic: same three-column layout; venetoclax bar appears with 'BCL-2 only' label; CLL resolves to 'DIES' (teal therapeutic chip); Platelet resolves to 'SURVIVES' (teal chip — window opened); Neutrophil 'SURVIVES' (teal chip)

beats.narration_text: The therapeutic window was not set by anti-tumor potency. It was set by which normal cell shared the cancer's guardian dependency. Redesigning around that dependency opened the window.

card.copy: The therapeutic window is set by which normal cell shares the cancer's guardian dependency.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: This is part of the Cancer Biology series from Humanitarians AI.

props.tagline: AI education for humanitarian and social-impact practitioners.
